{"key_event":"Dervish Taleh power amid British coastal withdrawal","reasoning":"British coastal concentration and thin Italian indirect rule left most inland areas governed by clans, customary authorities, and the Dervish movement, so civil society/clan autonomy rose modestly despite wartime trade disruption and Dervish coercion against rivals. Overall state power increased only slightly: colonial states remained weak and partitioned, but the Dervish quasi-state, Camel Corps, Italian local consolidation, and late-1919 British preparations added more organized coercive capacity than in the previous decade.","society_change":0.5,"society_power":5.5,"state_change":0.5,"state_power":3.0}
